pillars.name: Nuclear Structure
pillars.description: The composition and organisation of the atomic nucleus: protons and neutrons, binding energy, nuclear size and shape, and the models that explain nuclear stability.
pillars.descriptionCs: Složení a uspořádání atomového jádra: protony a neutrony, vazebná energie, velikost a tvar jádra a modely vysvětlující jadernou stabilitu.

blocks.name: Nucleons & Binding Energy
blocks.description: The nucleus is bound protons and neutrons held by the strong nuclear force; the binding energy per nucleon peaks near iron, explaining why fusion of light nuclei and fission of heavy ones both release energy.
blocks.descriptionCs: Jádro tvoří vázané protony a neutrony držené silnou jadernou silou; vazebná energie na nukleon dosahuje maxima poblíž železa, což vysvětluje, proč fúze lehkých jader i štěpení těžkých uvolňují energii.

brief.name: Nucleon
brief.definition: A proton or neutron, the constituents of the nucleus.

brief.name: Strong nuclear force
brief.definition: The short-range attraction binding nucleons against electrostatic repulsion.

brief.name: Binding energy
brief.definition: The energy needed to disassemble a nucleus, reflected in a mass deficit.

brief.name: Mass defect
brief.definition: The difference between a nucleus's mass and the sum of its free nucleons.

brief.name: Binding-energy curve
brief.definition: The variation of binding energy per nucleon with mass number, peaking at iron.

blocks.name: Nuclear Models
blocks.description: Complementary models capture nuclear behaviour: the liquid-drop model explains binding and fission energetics, while the shell model explains magic numbers and spins through nucleons filling quantised energy levels.
blocks.descriptionCs: Doplňující se modely popisují chování jádra: kapkový model vysvětluje vazebnou energii a energetiku štěpení, zatímco slupkový model vysvětluje magická čísla a spiny pomocí nukleonů zaplňujících kvantované energetické hladiny.

brief.name: Liquid-drop model
brief.definition: A model treating the nucleus as an incompressible charged drop.

brief.name: Semi-empirical mass formula
brief.definition: The formula estimating binding energy from volume, surface, Coulomb and other terms.

brief.name: Shell model
brief.definition: A model of nucleons in quantised orbitals explaining stability patterns.

brief.name: Magic numbers
brief.definition: Nucleon counts giving closed shells and exceptional stability.

brief.name: Nuclear spin
brief.definition: The total angular momentum of a nucleus, important in magnetic resonance.

pillars.name: Radioactivity & Decay
pillars.description: The spontaneous transformation of unstable nuclei: alpha, beta and gamma decay, decay laws and half-lives, decay chains, and the applications of radioactive dating and tracing.
pillars.descriptionCs: Samovolná přeměna nestabilních jader: alfa, beta a gama rozpad, zákony rozpadu a poločasy, rozpadové řady a využití radioaktivního datování a značkování.

blocks.name: Modes of Radioactive Decay
blocks.description: Unstable nuclei shed energy by emitting an alpha particle, converting a neutron and proton through beta decay, or releasing a gamma photon; each mode changes the nucleus in a characteristic way governed by conservation laws.
blocks.descriptionCs: Nestabilní jádra se zbavují energie vyzářením částice alfa, přeměnou neutronu a protonu při beta rozpadu nebo uvolněním fotonu gama; každý způsob mění jádro charakteristickým způsobem daným zákony zachování.

brief.name: Alpha decay
brief.definition: Emission of a helium nucleus, reducing mass number by four.

brief.name: Beta decay
brief.definition: Conversion of a neutron to a proton or vice versa, emitting an electron or positron and a neutrino.

brief.name: Gamma decay
brief.definition: Emission of a high-energy photon as an excited nucleus relaxes.

brief.name: Electron capture
brief.definition: Absorption of an inner electron converting a proton to a neutron.

brief.name: Q-value
brief.definition: The energy released in a decay, shared as kinetic energy of the products.

blocks.name: Decay Law, Half-Life & Dating
blocks.description: Radioactive decay is a random process with a fixed probability per unit time, giving exponential decay and a characteristic half-life; the steady clock this provides underlies radiometric dating and radioactive tracing.
blocks.descriptionCs: Radioaktivní rozpad je náhodný děj s pevnou pravděpodobností za jednotku času, což vede k exponenciálnímu poklesu a charakteristickému poločasu rozpadu; tyto stálé hodiny jsou základem radiometrického datování a radioaktivního značkování.

brief.name: Half-life
brief.definition: The time for half the nuclei in a sample to decay.

brief.name: Exponential decay law
brief.definition: The rule that activity falls off exponentially with time.

brief.name: Activity
brief.definition: The number of decays per unit time in a radioactive sample.

brief.name: Decay chain
brief.definition: A sequence of decays leading from an unstable parent to a stable end nucleus.

brief.name: Radiometric dating
brief.definition: Determining age from the ratio of parent to daughter isotopes, as in carbon-14 dating.

pillars.name: Nuclear Reactions
pillars.description: Induced transformations of nuclei: reaction kinematics and cross sections, conservation laws, and the mechanisms of scattering, capture and transmutation.
pillars.descriptionCs: Vynucené přeměny jader: kinematika reakcí a účinné průřezy, zákony zachování a mechanismy rozptylu, záchytu a transmutace.

blocks.name: Reactions & Cross Sections
blocks.description: In a nuclear reaction incident particles transform a target nucleus, conserving nucleon number, charge, energy and momentum; the cross section quantifies reaction probability, peaking sharply at resonances.
blocks.descriptionCs: Při jaderné reakci dopadající částice přeměňují terčové jádro za zachování počtu nukleonů, náboje, energie a hybnosti; účinný průřez udává pravděpodobnost reakce a dosahuje ostrých maxim při rezonancích.

brief.name: Cross section
brief.definition: The effective target area measuring the probability of a nuclear reaction.

brief.name: Reaction kinematics
brief.definition: The application of energy and momentum conservation to reaction products.

brief.name: Compound nucleus
brief.definition: A short-lived excited nucleus formed and then decaying during a reaction.

brief.name: Resonance
brief.definition: A sharp peak in cross section when the energy matches a nuclear excited state.

brief.name: Transmutation
brief.definition: The conversion of one element into another by a nuclear reaction.

pillars.name: Fission, Fusion & Energy
pillars.description: The two routes to nuclear energy: the splitting of heavy nuclei and the merging of light ones, the physics of chain reactions and confinement, and their use in reactors and weapons.
pillars.descriptionCs: Dvě cesty k jaderné energii: štěpení těžkých jader a slučování lehkých, fyzika řetězových reakcí a udržení a jejich využití v reaktorech a zbraních.

blocks.name: Nuclear Fission
blocks.description: A heavy nucleus splits into lighter fragments, releasing energy and free neutrons that can sustain a chain reaction; controlled in reactors by moderators and control rods, uncontrolled it drives fission weapons.
blocks.descriptionCs: Těžké jádro se rozštěpí na lehčí fragmenty, přičemž uvolní energii a volné neutrony schopné udržet řetězovou reakci; v reaktorech se řídí moderátory a regulačními tyčemi, neřízené pohání štěpné zbraně.

brief.name: Induced fission
brief.definition: Splitting of a heavy nucleus triggered by neutron absorption.

brief.name: Chain reaction
brief.definition: A self-sustaining sequence in which fission neutrons induce further fissions.

brief.name: Critical mass
brief.definition: The minimum amount of fissile material needed to sustain a chain reaction.

brief.name: Moderator
brief.definition: A material slowing neutrons to increase the fission probability in a reactor.

brief.name: Fission products
brief.definition: The radioactive fragments and neutrons produced by fission.

blocks.name: Nuclear Fusion
blocks.description: Light nuclei merge into a heavier one, releasing large energy per nucleon but requiring immense temperature and pressure to overcome their electrostatic repulsion; fusion powers the stars and is pursued for terrestrial energy.
blocks.descriptionCs: Lehká jádra se slučují do těžšího a uvolňují velkou energii na nukleon, avšak vyžadují obrovskou teplotu a tlak k překonání elektrostatického odpuzování; fúze pohání hvězdy a usiluje se o ni jako o pozemský zdroj energie.

brief.name: Fusion reaction
brief.definition: The merging of light nuclei into a heavier one with energy release.

brief.name: Coulomb barrier
brief.definition: The electrostatic repulsion that fusing nuclei must overcome.

brief.name: Thermonuclear ignition
brief.definition: The condition where fusion becomes self-sustaining through its own heat.

brief.name: Magnetic confinement
brief.definition: Holding hot plasma with magnetic fields, as in a tokamak.

brief.name: Inertial confinement
brief.definition: Compressing fuel by lasers or beams to reach fusion conditions.

pillars.name: Applied & Astrophysical Nuclear Physics
pillars.description: Nuclear physics in practice and in the cosmos: nuclear medicine and radiation detection, radiation protection, and the nucleosynthesis that forges the elements in stars.
pillars.descriptionCs: Jaderná fyzika v praxi a ve vesmíru: nukleární medicína a detekce záření, radiační ochrana a nukleosyntéza, která ve hvězdách vytváří prvky.

blocks.name: Nuclear Medicine & Radiation Detection
blocks.description: Radioactive isotopes and radiation are harnessed for imaging and therapy and detected by instruments that convert ionisation into measurable signals; radiation protection limits exposure through shielding, distance and time.
blocks.descriptionCs: Radioaktivní izotopy a záření se využívají k zobrazování a léčbě a detekují je přístroje, které převádějí ionizaci na měřitelný signál; radiační ochrana omezuje ozáření pomocí stínění, vzdálenosti a času.

brief.name: Radiotracer
brief.definition: A radioactive label following a substance through a body or system.

brief.name: Positron emission tomography
brief.definition: Imaging that reconstructs positron-emitter distribution in the body.

brief.name: Radiotherapy
brief.definition: The use of ionising radiation to destroy diseased tissue.

brief.name: Geiger counter
brief.definition: A detector registering individual ionising events as pulses.

brief.name: Radiation protection
brief.definition: Limiting dose through shielding, distance and exposure time.

blocks.name: Nucleosynthesis
blocks.description: The chemical elements are forged by nuclear reactions: the lightest in the first minutes of the Big Bang, heavier ones by fusion in stellar cores, and the heaviest by rapid neutron capture in supernovae and neutron-star mergers.
blocks.descriptionCs: Chemické prvky vznikají jadernými reakcemi: nejlehčí v prvních minutách velkého třesku, těžší fúzí v jádrech hvězd a nejtěžší rychlým záchytem neutronů v supernovách a při splývání neutronových hvězd.

brief.name: Big Bang nucleosynthesis
brief.definition: The formation of hydrogen, helium and lithium in the early universe.

brief.name: Stellar nucleosynthesis
brief.definition: The building of elements up to iron by fusion in stars.

brief.name: s-process
brief.definition: Slow neutron capture building heavy nuclei in evolved stars.

brief.name: r-process
brief.definition: Rapid neutron capture forming the heaviest elements in explosive events.

brief.name: Cosmic abundance
brief.definition: The observed proportions of the elements set by these processes.
